facade_colour: red brick upper facade with dark trim over a shuttered black storefront

hcd_data.architectural_style: Converted House-form, Row (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that has been modified to accommodate a commercial conversion through a one-storey commercial addition. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. With its visibly layered built form, each property is also associated with the patterns of commercial adaptation and expansion that began in the early twentieth century. Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their brick cladding and shared roofline. 188, 190, 192, and 194 Baldwin Street have also retained their shared gables, polychrome brick detailing, and second storey window openings. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way WB | 30 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks & roadway | E-W commercial connector

city_data.residential_floors: All floors (1–2) – residential building on commercial land

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: red brick upper facade with dark trim over a shuttered black storefront
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Heavy graffiti and closed shutters dominate the street view, though the roof form and masonry remain readable.
photo_observations.window_details: Shuttered storefront opening below and a small upper opening set into the brick gable wall.
photo_observations.door_details: Commercial entry is integrated into the shuttered frontage.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple coping and fascia lines with no elaborate trim.
photo_observations.chimney_details: Two brick chimneys rise along the attached roofline.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Prominent steep roof and surviving brickwork remain the clearest heritage elements.
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: Steep front-gabled roof with visible chimneys
photo_observations.storefront_description: Closed storefront with heavy black-and-white graffiti on the shutter panels.
photo_observations.signage_observed: Tom’s Place awning is visible adjacent to the facade in the same row.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Ground floor commercial conversion with shuttered storefront, but upper brick facade with shared gable roofline and polychrome brick detailing intact, consistent with HCD-described converted house-form.
photo_observations.notes: HCD typology is 'Converted House-form, Row' -- the commercial ground floor IS the expected condition. Shared gables, polychrome brick detailing, and second storey window openings remain visible above the storefront, matching HCD description precisely.

storefront_description: Closed storefront with heavy black-and-white graffiti on the shutter panels.
notes: Converted House-form Row -- commercial ground floor is the expected HCD condition. Shared gables and polychrome brick intact.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a flat, consistent with its converted house-form, row typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 1, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their brick cladding and shared roofline. 188, 190, 192, and 194 Baldwin Street have also retained their shared gables, polychrome brick detailing, and second storey window openings.

facade_description: Two-storey red brick facade with a flat roof, approximately 5.2 m wide. Part of the pre-1889 converted house-form row at 188-194 Baldwin St. Photo observation shows a closed storefront with heavy black-and-white graffiti on shutter panels at grade, with the upper brick facade and steep front-gabled roof intact above. Two brick chimneys rise along the attached roofline. Asymmetric window arrangement with one opening per floor. The Tom's Place awning is visible adjacent. The shared gables and surviving brickwork above the storefront level remain the clearest heritage elements despite the neglected street-level condition.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties have evolved over time while retaining original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their brick cladding and shared roofline. 188, 190, 192, and 194 Baldwin Street have also retained their shared gables, polychrome brick detailing, and second storey window openings.
